wanted to use xor-expresions in sympify, but it did not work.

Not really a bug, but I realized I could not load `Xor` with `a ^ b` in sympify, as it is interpreted as `a**b`.

Is this intentional? 

```
import sympy

print(sympy.sympify('Xor(a, b)'))
print(sympy.sympify('a ^ b'))
print(sympy.sympify('a**b'))
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27336/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27336/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2513674940","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27336#issuecomment-2513674940","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27336","id":2513674940,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs6V06K8","user":{"login":"asmeurer","id":71486,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcxNDg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/71486?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer","html_url":"https://github.com/asmeurer","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-03T06:33:22Z","updated_at":"2024-12-03T06:33:22Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I wish we could deprecate and remove these non-Python things from sympify parsing.  set -x
 
+source $RECIPE_DIR/get_cpu_arch.sh
+
+mkdir gcc-jit
+pushd gcc-jit
+../gcc/configure \
+    --host=$HOST \
+    --target=$HOST \
+    --enable-host-shared \
+    --enable-languages=jit \
+    --disable-bootstrap \
+    --disable-multilib \
+    --enable-libquadmath \
+    --enable-libquadmath-support \
+    --enable-long-long \
+    --disable-libssp \
+    --disable-libmudflap \
+    --enable-gold \
+    --disable-nls \
+    --prefix=$PREFIX/lib/emacs/jit
+make -j"${CPU_COUNT}"
+make install-strip
+cp -s "$PREFIX"/lib/emacs/jit/bin/x86_64-conda-linux-gnu-gcc-"$GCC_VERSION" "$PREFIX"/bin/","path":"recipe/build.sh","commit_id":"3a8a5b1b91c4384911513963f338d9ec3a14e777","original_commit_id":"3a8a5b1b91c4384911513963f338d9ec3a14e777","user":{"login":"asmeurer","id":71486,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcxNDg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/71486?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer","html_url":"https://github.com/asmeurer","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Does this work on Mac too? with libgccjit in lib/emacs/jit

--

As including libgccjit in gcc feedstock seems unlikely, I added libgccjit directly to emacs. This is a bit dirty solution, and significantly increases the package size. On the other hand, this way emacs does not require a particular gcc version like it would with the proper gccjit-in-gcc-package approach, which is handy for C/C++ development.

I am using this build now on linux64, did not test the cross compilation yet (so it probably does not with libgccjit in lib/emacs/jit

--

As including libgccjit in gcc feedstock seems unlikely, I added libgccjit directly to emacs. This is a bit dirty solution, and significantly increases the package size. On the other hand, this way emacs does not require a particular gcc version like it would with the proper gccjit-in-gcc-package approach, which is handy for C/C++ development.

I am using this build now on linux64, did not test the cross compilation yet (so it probably does not with libgccjit in lib/emacs/jit

--

As including libgccjit in gcc feedstock seems unlikely, I added libgccjit directly to emacs. This is a bit dirty solution, and significantly increases the package size. On the other hand, this way emacs does not require a particular gcc version like it would with the proper gccjit-in-gcc-package approach, which is handy for C/C++ development.

I am using this build now on linux64, did not test the cross compilation yet (so it probably does not with libgccjit in lib/emacs/jit

--

As including libgccjit in gcc feedstock seems unlikely, I added libgccjit directly to emacs. This is a bit dirty solution, and significantly increases the package size. On the other hand, this way emacs does not require a particular gcc version like it would with the proper gccjit-in-gcc-package approach, which is handy for C/C++ development.

I am using this build now on linux64, did not test the cross compilation yet (so it probably does not with libgccjit in lib/emacs/jit

--

As including libgccjit in gcc feedstock seems unlikely, I added libgccjit directly to emacs. This is a bit dirty solution, and significantly increases the package size. On the other hand, this way emacs does not require a particular gcc version like it would with the proper gccjit-in-gcc-package approach, which is handy for C/C++ development.

I am using this build now on linux64, did not test the cross compilation yet (so it probably does not with libgccjit in lib/emacs/jit

--

As including libgccjit in gcc feedstock seems unlikely, I added libgccjit directly to emacs. This is a bit dirty solution, and significantly increases the package size. On the other hand, this way emacs does not require a particular gcc version like it would with the proper gccjit-in-gcc-package approach, which is handy for C/C++ development.

I am using this build now on linux64, did not test the cross compilation yet (so it probably does not with libgccjit in lib/emacs/jit

--

As including libgccjit in gcc feedstock seems unlikely, I added libgccjit directly to emacs. This is a bit dirty solution, and significantly increases the package size. On the other hand, this way emacs does not require a particular gcc version like it would with the proper gccjit-in-gcc-package approach, which is handy for C/C++ development.

I am using this build now on linux64, did not test the cross compilation yet (so it probably does not work).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/emacs-feedstock/issues/89/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/emacs-feedstock/issues/89/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/emacs-feedstock/issues/comments/2525332687","html_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/emacs-feedstock/pull/89#issuecomment-2525332687","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/emacs-feedstock/issues/89","id":2525332687,"node_id":"IC_kwDOBB3Aic6WhYTP","user":{"login":"asmeurer","id":71486,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcxNDg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/71486?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer","html_url":"https://github.com/asmeurer","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-07T22:43:59Z","updated_at":"2024-12-07T22:43:59Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"It might be helpful to add some comments to the build files to clarify what is going on for future reference.

with libgccjit in lib/emacs/jit

--

As including libgccjit in gcc feedstock seems unlikely, I added libgccjit directly to emacs. This is a bit dirty solution, and significantly increases the package size. On the other hand, this way emacs does not require a particular gcc version like it would with the proper gccjit-in-gcc-package approach, which is handy for C/C++ development.

I am using this build now on linux64, did not test the cross compilation yet (so it probably does not with libgccjit in lib/emacs/jit

--

As including libgccjit in gcc feedstock seems unlikely, I added libgccjit directly to emacs. This is a bit dirty solution, and significantly increases the package size. On the other hand, this way emacs does not require a particular gcc version like it would with the proper gccjit-in-gcc-package approach, which is handy for C/C++ development.

 set -x
 
+source $RECIPE_DIR/get_cpu_arch.sh
+
+mkdir gcc-jit
+pushd gcc-jit
+../gcc/configure \
+    --host=$HOST \
+    --target=$HOST \
+    --enable-host-shared \
+    --enable-languages=jit \
+    --disable-bootstrap \
+    --disable-multilib \
+    --enable-libquadmath \
+    --enable-libquadmath-support \
+    --enable-long-long \
+    --disable-libssp \
+    --disable-libmudflap \
+    --enable-gold \
+    --disable-nls \
+    --prefix=$PREFIX/lib/emacs/jit
+make -j"${CPU_COUNT}"
+make install-strip
+cp -s "$PREFIX"/lib/emacs/jit/bin/x86_64-conda-linux-gnu-gcc-"$GCC_VERSION" "$PREFIX"/bin/","path":"recipe/build.sh","commit_id":"e5d9a2aae91dc95b3e5b13b2d4dace9b4b815f34","original_commit_id":"3a8a5b1b91c4384911513963f338d9ec3a14e777","user":{"login":"asmeurer","id":71486,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcxNDg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/71486?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer","html_url":"https://github.com/asmeurer","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Supposedly it can be done and Homebrew does it https://old.reddit.com/r/emacs/comments/qehd3c/build_emacs_with_native_compilation_in_macos/?rdt=61773. with libgccjit in lib/emacs/jit

--

As including libgccjit in gcc feedstock seems unlikely, I added libgccjit directly to emacs. This is a bit dirty solution, and significantly increases the package size. On the other hand, this way emacs does not require a particular gcc version like it would with the proper gccjit-in-gcc-package approach, which is handy for C/C++ development.

I am using this build now on linux64, did not test the cross compilation yet (so it probably does not with libgccjit in lib/emacs/jit

--

As including libgccjit in gcc feedstock seems unlikely, I added libgccjit directly to emacs. This is a bit dirty solution, and significantly increases the package size. On the other hand, this way emacs does not require a particular gcc version like it would with the proper gccjit-in-gcc-package approach, which is handy for C/C++ development.

wanted to use xor-expresions in sympify, but it did not work.

Not really a bug, but I realized I could not load `Xor` with `a ^ b` in sympify, as it is interpreted as `a**b`.

Is this intentional? 

```
import sympy

print(sympy.sympify('Xor(a, b)'))
print(sympy.sympify('a ^ b'))
print(sympy.sympify('a**b'))
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27336/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27336/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2525340169","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27336#issuecomment-2525340169","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27336","id":2525340169,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs6WhaIJ","user":{"login":"asmeurer","id":71486,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcxNDg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/71486?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer","html_url":"https://github.com/asmeurer","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-07T23:17:49Z","updated_at":"2024-12-07T23:17:49Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Yes, to be clear, this would be a significant backwards compatibility break and it's not clear if it's actually worth doing. `sympify` used to be the only function that parsed strings into SymPy expressions, but then `parse_expr` was added, which also included a lot of support for non-Python syntax. Since then, it's been clearer that non-Python parsing things really belong in `parse_expr`, not `sympify`. Of course, things like xor parsing have been there since the beginning so actually doing this breaks things.

(with that being said, I do think it would be a good idea to remove the factorial and repeated decimal parsing from `sympify`, see https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/20625)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2525340169/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

extensions"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":11,"created_at":"2024-12-08T19:04:01Z","updated_at":"2024-12-10T06:18:07Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"COLLABORATOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"There was previous discussion about this in the PR gh-23181 (CC @eagleoflqj). I also have a related PR gh-27353.

The `symbols` function is impossible to use for downstream code that wants to use static typing. There are three basic problems that prevent it from working properly:

Firstly symbols returns different types depending on the value (rather than the type) of the string argument:
```python
In [1]: type(symbols('x, y'))
Out[1]: tuple

In [2]: type(symbols('x'))
Out[2]: sympy.core.symbol.Symbol
```
This makes it impossible to infer the types in the most common usage of the `symbols` function. The reason this is done is so that you can do:
```python
x = symbols('x')
x, y = symbols('x, y')
```
Otherwise you would need something like this for a single symbol:
```python
[x] = symbols('x')
x, = symbols('x')
```
Of course there is no reason why you need to use `symbols` for a single symbol but that usage is widespread including in the sympy codebase itself.

The second problem with symbols is that it can be used to create other types e.g.:
```python
f, g, h = symbols('f, g, h', cls=Function)
```
In principle this should be understandable to a type checker and I can write type annotations that pyright will understand but I have not succeeded in getting anything that mypy will understand if there is a default value `cls=Symbol`. Trying to make this work would be quite disruptive to users because it would end up meaning that they get errors from mypy any time they use the symbols function.

The third problem is just that it does all kinds of weird stuff like:
```python
In [7]: symbols(['x:3', 'y', 'z', [[['Q:2:2', 'z']]]])
Out[7]: [(x₀, x₁, x₂), y, z, [[[(Q₀₀, Q₀₁, Q₁₀, Q₁₁), z]]]]
```

In the PR gh-27353 I have added an overload that says that when using the parameter `seq=True` the return type will always be a tuple of Symbol:
```python
x, y, z = symbols('x, y, z', seq=True)
```
That makes is possible for both pyright and mypy to infer the types and can be used in downstream codebases that depend on sympy since it works with older sympy versions and can be understood by a type checker. I also used that in the sympy.abc module so that the symbols there have inferred types.

Using `seq=True` like this solves problem 1 because the type is unambiguous but it ignores problem 2 by assuming that it is always Symbol and it also ignores the weird types in problem three but I think that is okay. So this isn't ideal but I think it is good as a starting point that there is at least some well-typed way of calling `symbols` for the most common usage.

The main problems with the `seq=True` approach are:

1. I don't think we really want users to have to write `seq=True` although I think some will be happy to use it if it makes type checking possible.
2. It still assumes that it is always `Symbol` so e.g. `symbols('f, g, h', cls=Function)` would be interpreted incorrectly by a type checker.

I think that the proper solution is just that we need a new function that is suitable for anyone downstream to create symbols of different types in a statically typed context. I have a demonstration here of what I have done with the overloads in gh-27353 as well as adding a new function called `symbols_type` which can be called like `symbols_type('x, y, z', Function)`:
```python
from typing import (
    reveal_type,
    TypeVar,
    Callable,
    Any,
    ParamSpec,
    Concatenate,
    overload,
    Literal,
)

from collections.abc import Iterable
from sympy import Expr, Symbol, Function, MatrixSymbol

@overload
def symbols(names: str, *, cls: Any = Symbol, seq: Literal[False] = False,
    **kwargs: bool | int,) -> Any: ...
@overload
def symbols(names: str, *, cls: Any = Symbol, seq: Literal[True],
    **kwargs: bool | int,) -> tuple[Symbol, ...]: ...

def symbols(names: str, *, cls: Any = Symbol, seq=True, **kwargs) -> Any | tuple[Symbol, ...]:
    syms = tuple(cls(name, **kwargs) for name in names.split(", "))
    if not seq and len(syms) == 1:
        return syms[0]
    else:
        return syms


T = TypeVar("T")
P = ParamSpec("P")


def symbols_type(
    names: str | Iterable[str | tuple[str, int] | tuple[str, int, int]],
    func: Callable[Concatenate[str, P], T],
    *args: P.args,
    **kwargs: P.kwargs,
) -> tuple[T, ...]:
    """Make symbols of type T.

    >>> symbols_type('x, y, z', Symbol)
    (x, y, z)
    >>> symbols_type(['x', 'y', 'z'], Symbol)
    (x, y, z)

    Symbols of different types can be made:

    >>> symbols_type('f, g, h', Function)
    (f, g, h)
    >>> symbols_type('A, B, C', MatrixSymbol, 2, 2)
    (A, B, C)

    Keyword arguments such as assumptions for Symbol can be passed:

    >>> symbols_type('a, b, c', Symbol, integer=True)
    (a, b, c)

    Multiple symbols can be generated with numbered names:

    >>> symbols_type([('x', 3), ('y', 2)], Symbol)
    (x0, x1, x2, y0, y1)
    >>> symbols_type([('x', 3, 6)], Symbol)
    (x3, x4, x5)
    """

    match names:
        case str():
            names_list = [n.strip() for n in names.split(",") for n in n.split()]
        case Iterable():
            names_list = [n for item in names for n in _parse_item(item)]
        case _:
            raise TypeError("Expecting str or iterable")

    return tuple(func(name, *args, **kwargs) for name in names_list)


def _parse_item(item: str | tuple[str, int] | tuple[str, int, int]) -> list[str]:
    """Handle e.g. ('x', 3) -> ['x0', 'x1', 'x2']"""
    match item:
        case str(name):
            return [name]
        case (str(name), int(stop)):
            return [name + str(i) for i in range(stop)]
        case (str(name), int(start), int(stop)):
            return [name + str(i) for i in range(start, stop)]
        case _:
            raise TypeError("Expected str, (str,int), or (str,int,int)")


a, b, c = symbols("a, b, c")
x, y, z = symbols("x, y, z", seq=True)
i, j, k = symbols_type("i, j, k", Symbol)
f, g, h = symbols_type("f, g, h", Function)
A, B, C = symbols_type("A, B, C", MatrixSymbol, 2, 2)

reveal_type(a)  # Any
reveal_type(x)  # Symbol
reveal_type(i)  # Symbol
reveal_type(f)  # type[AppliedUndef]
reveal_type(f(x))  # Expr
reveal_type(A)  # MatrixSymbol


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import doctest

    doctest.testmod()
```
My suggestion would be to leave the existing `symbols` function as is apart from adding hints for `seq=True` and then add a new function suitable for downstream use and also that should be mostly used throughout the sympy codebase. Here I have suggested calling the function `symbols_type` because the idea is that you pass the type of symbol that you want as the second argument. Other names like `syms` etc could be used.

Using positional arguments for the type means we can pass additional positional arguments to the symbol constructor e.g.:
```python
In [2]: A, B, C = symbols_type("A, B, C", MatrixSymbol, 2, 2)

In [3]: A.shape
Out[3]: (2, 2)
```
This is something that needs a `lambda` with `symbols`:
```python
In [5]: A, B, C = symbols("A, B, C", cls=lambda n: MatrixSymbol(n, 2, 2))
```
I don't think that it is possible with `symbols_type` to have a default argument for the type i.e. to have it default to `Symbol` without complaints from mypy. Maybe it is possible and someone else can figure that out but it is really just a deficiency in mypy because there are many ways to get pyright to understand this. For now though we could add something like `symbols_type` that has a non-optional second argument so to use it with Symbol it would be `symbols_type('x, y, z', Symbol)` or perhaps `syms('x, y, z', Symbol)`.

The implementation that I showed allows creating multiple symbols using range syntax like `symbols_type([('x', 10)], Symbol)`. I think that is better than `symbols('x:10')`  because often I want to create `n` symbols where `n` is a Python variable and it is awkward to format that into a string. I don't think that it is worth supporting any of the other cases that `symbols` handles. ultimately in more complicated cases you should just use a loop or something because the purpose of symbols is just to be convenient for the common case where all the names are spelled out.

Obviously there are many possibilities for what the name could be and what sort of options and arguments it should take but I think that many users are going to want a function that works with type It would be great if there was some way like a macro that could make it possible to avoid duplicating the symbol names and variable names like in Matlab or Julia:

To be fair that already exists, the `var` function.

```py
>>> var('x y z') # x, y, and z are now defined
```

It also uses a hack but not nearly as hacky as adding new syntax (just injecting names into globals; which static checkers do not like).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2530544825/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

with libgccjit in lib/emacs/jit

--

As including libgccjit in gcc feedstock seems unlikely, I added libgccjit directly to emacs. This is a bit dirty solution, and significantly increases the package size. On the other hand, this way emacs does not require a particular gcc version like it would with the proper gccjit-in-gcc-package approach, which is handy for C/C++ development.

I am using this build now on linux64, did not test the cross compilation yet (so it probably does not with libgccjit in lib/emacs/jit

--

As including libgccjit in gcc feedstock seems unlikely, I added libgccjit directly to emacs. This is a bit dirty solution, and significantly increases the package size. On the other hand, this way emacs does not require a particular gcc version like it would with the proper gccjit-in-gcc-package approach, which is handy for C/C++ development.

I am using this build now on linux64, did not test the cross compilation yet (so it probably does not symptom occurs only in IDE environment such as Spyder or Atom, where the formula image is rendered by latex.

When I start using sympy with `from sympy import *`, everything's just fine. Formulae are shown nicely.

But when I run `init_printing()`, formulae images are not displayed but result an infinite loop.

I believe something is going wrong when sympy tries to use the LaTeX program, which is installed in my computer because:

1. When I do the same job on my labtob, where LaTeX is not installed, nothing goes wrong.

2. When I pass 'mathjax', 'matplotlib', or 'svg' to `use_latex` parameter of `init_printing()`, this symptom does not happen. --

As including libgccjit in gcc feedstock seems unlikely, I added libgccjit directly to emacs. This is a bit dirty solution, and significantly increases the package size. On the other hand, this way emacs does not require a particular gcc version like it would with the proper gccjit-in-gcc-package approach, which is handy for C/C++ development.

should investigate if adding type hints to the codebase can help us. 

I don't have any further thoughts on how this should look like or even if it's a good idea. I haven't really used type hints much myself. I know that they can be useful for documentation, but I don't know if any of the static type checking tools can do much with SymPy, given how dynamic the code is. Also most types in SymPy would just be "Expr", or maybe "Symbol". But perhaps it can be of some real use. Please discuss it here if you have any thoughts.

It will probably be better to not merge anything until https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/17943 is fixed (until we drop Python 2 testing from Travis). That way we can use the Python 3 syntax for type hints. However, if someone wants to work on this before then, you can start a PR that just won't be merged until later. We are way past the point where type hints are something that we can just "look into". The entire Python community/ecosystem has moved on without us and now expects type hints everywhere. It is very clear now that while type hints can be optional for end users they are not optional for libraries like SymPy which already looks backwards in this respect compared to every other core library in the scientific Python ecosystem.

I agree, and also we now do have some type hints. So what should be considered a resolution of this issue? Sufficient type hints for mypy to pass?  Is this issue just about a discussion about how far we should go into type hinting?

Or should we close this issue and open new more targeted ones for specific parts of the codebase that need type hints? It also feels like most libraries don't particularly care about making type hints readable. 

For the former, better tooling is possible. For instance, here's how to show a function signature without type annotations:

```py
def signature_no_annotations(func):
    import inspect
    s = inspect.signature(func)
    func_name = func.__name__
    args = s.replace(parameters=[p.replace(annotation=inspect.Signature.empty) for p in s.parameters.values()], return_annotation=inspect.Signature.empty)
    return func_name + str(args)
```

```py
>>> def f(a: int) -> int:
...     pass
>>> signature_no_annotations(f)
'f(a)'
```

It's just up to toolking to offer this option. 

To the latter, we really can (and should) do better than many libraries by using things like type aliases and more concise typing syntax. should investigate if adding type hints to the codebase can help us. 

I don't have any further thoughts on how this should look like or even if it's a good idea. I haven't really used type hints much myself. I know that they can be useful for documentation, but I don't know if any of the static type checking tools can do much with SymPy, given how dynamic the code is. Also most types in SymPy would just be "Expr", or maybe "Symbol". But perhaps it can be of some real use. Please discuss it here if you have any thoughts.

It will probably be better to not merge anything until https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/17943 is fixed (until we drop Python 2 testing from Travis). That way we can use the Python 3 syntax for type hints. However, if someone wants to work on this before then, you can start a PR that just won't be merged until later. I just want type checkers for downstream code to be able to infer the types e.g. this is what pyright currently gives:

Focusing on end users instead of SymPy itself seems like a good goal for right now. 

One question though, is if we have something like

```py
@overload
def f(x: Expr) -> Expr: ...

@overload
def f(x: int) -> Integer: ...
```

How do we check that it's actually correct (that `f` always returns an integer when given an integer)? Does the type checker verify it? Or do we need to make sure we have tests for it (this would be a good use-case for hypothesis https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/20914)? It would be easy to get an overload like this wrong based on a faulty assumption, especially once you start overloading types like `Add` and `Mul`. there,

I'm working with an [algorithm](https://github.com/SymposiumOrganization/NeuralSymbolicRegressionThatScales) that relies on calling `sympy.lambdify` hundreds of millions of times (~200M) and noticed the memory usage of the process steadily creeping up and eventually crashing the program.

I was able to trace the problem to the following (see **Demo** below).

Notably, the memory usage still increases despite
1. lambdifying the same equation again
2. "deleting" the lambdified equation

**System Information:**
WSL2 (Windows 10.0.22631 Build 22631)

```
> lsb_release -a
No LSB modules are available.
Distributor ID: Ubuntu
Description:    Ubuntu 22.04.3 LTS
Release:        22.04
Codename:       jammy
```
```
> python --version
Python 3.11.10
```
```
> pip freeze
mpmath==1.3.0
psutil==6.1.0
sympy==1.13.3
```


### Demo:


```python
# sympy_memory.py
import argparse
import psutil

import sympy


if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Parse command line arguments
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Run equation n times')
    parser.add_argument('-n', type=int, default=1, help='Number of repetitions')
    args = parser.parse_args()

    # Get the current process to monitor memory usage
    process = psutil.Process()

    # Parse an example equation
    equation_str = 'x'
    parsed_equation = sympy.sympify(equation_str)

    print("Memory Usage:")

    for i in range(args.n):
        # Lambdifying the (same) equation in each iteration
        lambdified_equation = sympy.lambdify('x', parsed_equation)

        # Print memory usage every 10000 iterations
        if i % 10000 == 0:
            mem_usage = process.memory_info().rss / 1024 / 1024  # Convert to MB
            print(f"{i}/{args.n}: {mem_usage:.2f} MB")

        # "Deleting" the lambdified equation
        del lambdified_equation
```

```
> python sympy_memory.py -n 100000
Memory Usage:
0/100000: 49.91 MB
10000/100000: 54.29 MB
20000/100000: 59.27 MB
30000/100000: 64.17 MB
40000/100000: 68.04 MB
50000/100000: 72.05 MB
60000/100000: 75.91 MB
70000/100000: 79.78 MB
80000/100000: 83.45 MB
90000/100000: 89.08 MB
```

**Observation:** The memory usage increases by about ~~+390KB~~ (correction: 390B https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27216#issuecomment-2453168997) per lambdified equation, totalling +39MB over 100k lambdifications and deletions.

But for lambdify that would either mean just storing the lines elsewhere in the function closure, or recomputing lambdify in it. The latter could be bug prone if any part of lambdify relied on some state that changed since the original call. 

I think it should be possible to fix this by using `weakref.finalize` https://docs.python.org/3/library/weakref.html#weakref.finalize to clear the linecache entry whenever the function is garbage collected. (it would also be nice if linecache supported this directly; maybe someone can open an issue/PR to CPython)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2458054619/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

there,

I'm working with an [algorithm](https://github.com/SymposiumOrganization/NeuralSymbolicRegressionThatScales) that relies on calling `sympy.lambdify` hundreds of millions of times (~200M) and noticed the memory usage of the process steadily creeping up and eventually crashing the program.

I was able to trace the problem to the following (see **Demo** below).

Notably, the memory usage still increases despite
1. lambdifying the same equation again
2. "deleting" the lambdified equation

**System Information:**
WSL2 (Windows 10.0.22631 Build 22631)

```
> lsb_release -a
No LSB modules are available.
Distributor ID: Ubuntu
Description:    Ubuntu 22.04.3 LTS
Release:        22.04
Codename:       jammy
```
```
> python --version
Python 3.11.10
```
```
> pip freeze
mpmath==1.3.0
psutil==6.1.0
sympy==1.13.3
```


### Demo:


```python
# sympy_memory.py
import argparse
import psutil

import sympy


if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Parse command line arguments
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Run equation n times')
    parser.add_argument('-n', type=int, default=1, help='Number of repetitions')
    args = parser.parse_args()

    # Get the current process to monitor memory usage
    process = psutil.Process()

    # Parse an example equation
    equation_str = 'x'
    parsed_equation = sympy.sympify(equation_str)

    print("Memory Usage:")

    for i in range(args.n):
        # Lambdifying the (same) equation in each iteration
        lambdified_equation = sympy.lambdify('x', parsed_equation)

        # Print memory usage every 10000 iterations
        if i % 10000 == 0:
            mem_usage = process.memory_info().rss / 1024 / 1024  # Convert to MB
            print(f"{i}/{args.n}: {mem_usage:.2f} MB")

        # "Deleting" the lambdified equation
        del lambdified_equation
```

```
> python sympy_memory.py -n 100000
Memory Usage:
0/100000: 49.91 MB
10000/100000: 54.29 MB
20000/100000: 59.27 MB
30000/100000: 64.17 MB
40000/100000: 68.04 MB
50000/100000: 72.05 MB
60000/100000: 75.91 MB
70000/100000: 79.78 MB
80000/100000: 83.45 MB
90000/100000: 89.08 MB
```

**Observation:** The memory usage increases by about ~~+390KB~~ (correction: 390B https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27216#issuecomment-2453168997) per lambdified equation, totalling +39MB over 100k lambdifications and deletions.

quality","color":"cccccc","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":124,"created_at":"2019-11-21T22:45:08Z","updated_at":"2024-11-05T21:45:22Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"We should investigate if adding type hints to the codebase can help us. 

I don't have any further thoughts on how this should look like or even if it's a good idea. I haven't really used type hints much myself. I know that they can be useful for documentation, but I don't know if any of the static type checking tools can do much with SymPy, given how dynamic the code is. Also most types in SymPy would just be "Expr", or maybe "Symbol". But perhaps it can be of some real use. Please discuss it here if you have any thoughts.

It will probably be better to not merge anything until https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/17943 is fixed (until we drop Python 2 testing from Travis). That way we can use the Python 3 syntax for type hints. However, if someone wants to work on this before then, you can start a PR that just won't be merged until later. The input to a function would already be evaluated to one or the other (that's assuming we ignore unevaluated expressions, which are probably going to be a huge pain for type checking).

The point is it could (presumably) be useful for a type checker to know something like `expr.as_coeff_Mul()` always returns `(S.One, expr)` when `expr` is an `Add` (this is maybe too trivial of an example since the checker could deduce this trivially from the [source of `Expr.as_coeff_Mul`](https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/d293133e81194adc11177729af91c970f092a6e7/sympy/core/expr.py#L3546), but this sort of thing applies in many places). /matplotlib/pull/28187.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This allows specifying the output base name of the generated image files. The name can include '{counter}', which is automatically string formatted to an incrementing counter. The default if it is not specified is left intact as the current behavior, which is to use the base name of the provided script or the RST document.

This is required to use the plot directive with MyST, because the directive is broken with MyST (an issue I don't want to fix), requiring the use of eval-rst. But the way eval-rst works, the incrementing counter is not maintained across different eval-rst directives, meaning if you try to include multiple of them in the same document, the images will overwrite each other. This allows you to manually work around this with something like

````
```{eval-rst}
.. plot::
   :output-base-name: plot-1

   ...
```

```{eval-rst}
.. plot::
   :output-base-name: plot-2

   ...
```
````

Aside from this, it's generally useful to be able to specify the image name used for a plot, as a more informative name can be used rather than just `<document-name>-1.png`.

Note: I've done minimal testing of this, but more extensive testing would probably be a good idea.

Although that function isn't actually called that often (https://github.com/search?q=repo%3Apython%2Fcpython+checkcache+language%3APython&type=code&l=Python), so we would still probably want to call it ourselves in the finalizer to avoid the memleak. The way `checkcache` is written now though that seems impossible. So I don't see how to fix this short of monkeypatching something in `linecache`. 

More generally, better upstream support for dynamically generated linecache entries would be nice. It feels like they're only unofficially supported right now. This feature also gets used by custom interpreters like IPython/Jupyter (although for IPython it's generally not a problem that the cache never gets cleared). 

Just as an aside: just what is the status of PyPy? It seems like they are struggling just to keep up with the oldest supported versions of CPython. I'm not necessarily suggesting we should drop support for it, but I am curious if it will stay relevant in the future. there,

I'm working with an [algorithm](https://github.com/SymposiumOrganization/NeuralSymbolicRegressionThatScales) that relies on calling `sympy.lambdify` hundreds of millions of times (~200M) and noticed the memory usage of the process steadily creeping up and eventually crashing the program.

I was able to trace the problem to the following (see **Demo** below).

Notably, the memory usage still increases despite
1. lambdifying the same equation again
2. "deleting" the lambdified equation

**System Information:**
WSL2 (Windows 10.0.22631 Build 22631)

```
> lsb_release -a
No LSB modules are available.
Distributor ID: Ubuntu
Description:    Ubuntu 22.04.3 LTS
Release:        22.04
Codename:       jammy
```
```
> python --version
Python 3.11.10
```
```
> pip freeze
mpmath==1.3.0
psutil==6.1.0
sympy==1.13.3
```


### Demo:


```python
# sympy_memory.py
import argparse
import psutil

import sympy


if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Parse command line arguments
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Run equation n times')
    parser.add_argument('-n', type=int, default=1, help='Number of repetitions')
    args = parser.parse_args()

    # Get the current process to monitor memory usage
    process = psutil.Process()

    # Parse an example equation
    equation_str = 'x'
    parsed_equation = sympy.sympify(equation_str)

    print("Memory Usage:")

    for i in range(args.n):
        # Lambdifying the (same) equation in each iteration
        lambdified_equation = sympy.lambdify('x', parsed_equation)

        # Print memory usage every 10000 iterations
        if i % 10000 == 0:
            mem_usage = process.memory_info().rss / 1024 / 1024  # Convert to MB
            print(f"{i}/{args.n}: {mem_usage:.2f} MB")

        # "Deleting" the lambdified equation
        del lambdified_equation
```

```
> python sympy_memory.py -n 100000
Memory Usage:
0/100000: 49.91 MB
10000/100000: 54.29 MB
20000/100000: 59.27 MB
30000/100000: 64.17 MB
40000/100000: 68.04 MB
50000/100000: 72.05 MB
60000/100000: 75.91 MB
70000/100000: 79.78 MB
80000/100000: 83.45 MB
90000/100000: 89.08 MB
```

**Observation:** The memory usage increases by about ~~+390KB~~ (correction: 390B https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27216#issuecomment-2453168997) per lambdified equation, totalling +39MB over 100k lambdifications and deletions.

2. It allows Jupyter tracebacks to show the source code:

    ```py

   In [1]: from sympy import *

   In [2]: x = symbols('x')

   In [3]: f = lambdify(x, tan(x) - sin(x))

   In [4]: f('x')
   ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
   Cell In[4], line 1
   ----> 1 f('x')

   File <lambdifygenerated-1>:2, in _lambdifygenerated(x)
         1 def _lambdifygenerated(x):
   ----> 2     return -sin(x) + tan(x)

   TypeError: ufunc 'sin' not supported for the input types, and the inputs could not be safely coerced to any supported types according to the casting rule ''safe''
   ```

  (unfortunately this doesn't happen for tracebacks in the normal interpreter because those don't use the linecache and they are impossible to hook into)
3. there,

I'm working with an [algorithm](https://github.com/SymposiumOrganization/NeuralSymbolicRegressionThatScales) that relies on calling `sympy.lambdify` hundreds of millions of times (~200M) and noticed the memory usage of the process steadily creeping up and eventually crashing the program.

I was able to trace the problem to the following (see **Demo** below).

Notably, the memory usage still increases despite
1. lambdifying the same equation again
2. "deleting" the lambdified equation

**System Information:**
WSL2 (Windows 10.0.22631 Build 22631)

```
> lsb_release -a
No LSB modules are available.
Distributor ID: Ubuntu
Description:    Ubuntu 22.04.3 LTS
Release:        22.04
Codename:       jammy
```
```
> python --version
Python 3.11.10
```
```
> pip freeze
mpmath==1.3.0
psutil==6.1.0
sympy==1.13.3
```


### Demo:


```python
# sympy_memory.py
import argparse
import psutil

import sympy


if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Parse command line arguments
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Run equation n times')
    parser.add_argument('-n', type=int, default=1, help='Number of repetitions')
    args = parser.parse_args()

    # Get the current process to monitor memory usage
    process = psutil.Process()

    # Parse an example equation
    equation_str = 'x'
    parsed_equation = sympy.sympify(equation_str)

    print("Memory Usage:")

    for i in range(args.n):
        # Lambdifying the (same) equation in each iteration
        lambdified_equation = sympy.lambdify('x', parsed_equation)

        # Print memory usage every 10000 iterations
        if i % 10000 == 0:
            mem_usage = process.memory_info().rss / 1024 / 1024  # Convert to MB
            print(f"{i}/{args.n}: {mem_usage:.2f} MB")

        # "Deleting" the lambdified equation
        del lambdified_equation
```

```
> python sympy_memory.py -n 100000
Memory Usage:
0/100000: 49.91 MB
10000/100000: 54.29 MB
20000/100000: 59.27 MB
30000/100000: 64.17 MB
40000/100000: 68.04 MB
50000/100000: 72.05 MB
60000/100000: 75.91 MB
70000/100000: 79.78 MB
80000/100000: 83.45 MB
90000/100000: 89.08 MB
```

**Observation:** The memory usage increases by about ~~+390KB~~ (correction: 390B https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27216#issuecomment-2453168997) per lambdified equation, totalling +39MB over 100k lambdifications and deletions.

there,

I'm working with an [algorithm](https://github.com/SymposiumOrganization/NeuralSymbolicRegressionThatScales) that relies on calling `sympy.lambdify` hundreds of millions of times (~200M) and noticed the memory usage of the process steadily creeping up and eventually crashing the program.

I was able to trace the problem to the following (see **Demo** below).

Notably, the memory usage still increases despite
1. lambdifying the same equation again
2. "deleting" the lambdified equation

**System Information:**
WSL2 (Windows 10.0.22631 Build 22631)

### Demo:


```python
# sympy_memory.py
import argparse
import psutil

import sympy


if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Parse command line arguments
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Run equation n times')
    parser.add_argument('-n', type=int, default=1, help='Number of repetitions')
    args = parser.parse_args()

    # Get the current process to monitor memory usage
    process = psutil.Process()

    # Parse an example equation
    equation_str = 'x'
    parsed_equation = sympy.sympify(equation_str)

    print("Memory Usage:")

    for i in range(args.n):
        # Lambdifying the (same) equation in each iteration
        lambdified_equation = sympy.lambdify('x', parsed_equation)

        # Print memory usage every 10000 iterations
        if i % 10000 == 0:
            mem_usage = process.memory_info().rss / 1024 / 1024  # Convert to MB
            print(f"{i}/{args.n}: {mem_usage:.2f} MB")

        # "Deleting" the lambdified equation
        del lambdified_equation
```

```
> python sympy_memory.py -n 100000
Memory Usage:
0/100000: 49.91 MB
10000/100000: 54.29 MB
20000/100000: 59.27 MB
30000/100000: 64.17 MB
40000/100000: 68.04 MB
50000/100000: 72.05 MB
60000/100000: 75.91 MB
70000/100000: 79.78 MB
80000/100000: 83.45 MB
90000/100000: 89.08 MB
```

**Observation:** The memory usage increases by about ~~+390KB~~ (correction: 390B https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27216#issuecomment-2453168997) per lambdified equation, totalling +39MB over 100k lambdifications and deletions.

We don't want to literally add `import` lines to the function, because that would be an unnecessary runtime performance hit. Maybe we could add it somehow to the docstring or elsewhere. Right now, the best way to tell what's going on is to step into the lambdified function in a debugger (which again is easier when the debugger can "see" the source) and analyze the namespace interactively. Ideally you'd want to see the code as something like

```
from numpy import *

def lambdifygenerated(x):
    return ...
```

since this is more or less what's actually happening. I don't know if the linecache lets you add a whole module's source code, but maybe this can be done. 

(as a complete aside: I wonder if the runtime performance is affected by the fact that lambdify adds all possible names to the globals instead of just the ones it needs. Maybe worth playing around with)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2466962785/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

there,

I'm working with an [algorithm](https://github.com/SymposiumOrganization/NeuralSymbolicRegressionThatScales) that relies on calling `sympy.lambdify` hundreds of millions of times (~200M) and noticed the memory usage of the process steadily creeping up and eventually crashing the program.

I was able to trace the problem to the following (see **Demo** below).

Notably, the memory usage still increases despite
1. lambdifying the same equation again
2. "deleting" the lambdified equation

**System Information:**
WSL2 (Windows 10.0.22631 Build 22631)

```
> lsb_release -a
No LSB modules are available.
Distributor ID: Ubuntu
Description:    Ubuntu 22.04.3 LTS
Release:        22.04
Codename:       jammy
```
```
> python --version
Python 3.11.10
```
```
> pip freeze
mpmath==1.3.0
psutil==6.1.0
sympy==1.13.3
```


### Demo:


```python
# sympy_memory.py
import argparse
import psutil

import sympy


if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Parse command line arguments
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Run equation n times')
    parser.add_argument('-n', type=int, default=1, help='Number of repetitions')
    args = parser.parse_args()

    # Get the current process to monitor memory usage
    process = psutil.Process()

    # Parse an example equation
    equation_str = 'x'
    parsed_equation = sympy.sympify(equation_str)

    print("Memory Usage:")

    for i in range(args.n):
        # Lambdifying the (same) equation in each iteration
        lambdified_equation = sympy.lambdify('x', parsed_equation)

        # Print memory usage every 10000 iterations
        if i % 10000 == 0:
            mem_usage = process.memory_info().rss / 1024 / 1024  # Convert to MB
            print(f"{i}/{args.n}: {mem_usage:.2f} MB")

        # "Deleting" the lambdified equation
        del lambdified_equation
```

```
> python sympy_memory.py -n 100000
Memory Usage:
0/100000: 49.91 MB
10000/100000: 54.29 MB
20000/100000: 59.27 MB
30000/100000: 64.17 MB
40000/100000: 68.04 MB
50000/100000: 72.05 MB
60000/100000: 75.91 MB
70000/100000: 79.78 MB
80000/100000: 83.45 MB
90000/100000: 89.08 MB
```

**Observation:** The memory usage increases by about ~~+390KB~~ (correction: 390B https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27216#issuecomment-2453168997) per lambdified equation, totalling +39MB over 100k lambdifications and deletions.

/matplotlib/pull/28187.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This allows specifying the output base name of the generated image files. The name can include '{counter}', which is automatically string formatted to an incrementing counter. The default if it is not specified is left intact as the current behavior, which is to use the base name of the provided script or the RST document.

This is required to use the plot directive with MyST, because the directive is broken with MyST (an issue I don't want to fix), requiring the use of eval-rst. But the way eval-rst works, the incrementing counter is not maintained across different eval-rst directives, meaning if you try to include multiple of them in the same document, the images will overwrite each other. This allows you to manually work around this with something like

````
```{eval-rst}
.. plot::
   :output-base-name: plot-1

   ...
```

```{eval-rst}
.. plot::
   :output-base-name: plot-2

   ...
```
````

Aside from this, it's generally useful to be able to specify the image name used for a plot, as a more informative name can be used rather than just `<document-name>-1.png`.

Note: I've done minimal testing of this, but more extensive testing would probably be a good idea.

API","user":{"login":"mmatera","id":2862288,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4NjIyODg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2862288?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera","html_url":"https://github.com/mmatera","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"#### References to other Issues or PRs

I am working on giving Mathics (https://github.com/Mathics3/mathics-core/pull/1155) the ability to produce prettyprint
-like output from Mathics `Expression` objects. To avoid duplicating efforts, I tried to hook on the `sympy.printing.pretty` package, but I found some problems. 
After trying to use the `PrettyPrinter`, I realized that since `mathics.core.Expression` objects behave differently than Sympy objects, the best would be to generate text blocks directly from the `sympy.printing.pretty.stringpict.prettyForm` class. A draft of it can be seen at https://github.com/Mathics3/mathics-core/pull/1162

In this journey, I found some issues in the design of `sympy.printing.pretty.stringpict` that makes me difficult to use it directly on my project. 
This PR includes the changes I need to make it work. Also, it probably helps with #20894.

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In this PR I propose some changes in the `stringPict` API, which I hope will make it easier to use it both inside Sympy  or in in other projects, like Mathics.

* the methods  `subscript` and `superscript` were added to the `stringPict` interface
* the methods `above`, `below`, `left`, `right` and `parens` were modified to return `prettyForm` or `stringPict`
   objects depending on the class of the object over which are called.
* `above`, `below`, `left`,  and `right`  now support a keyword argument `align` that allows to control the relative alignment of the elements.
* the `root` method was fixed, and used to simplify the code in `PrettyPrinter._print_nth_root()`.
* doctests for the modified methods were added.
* tests and the use of the modified methods were adjusted along the code.

The idea is that `stringPict.[above|below,left|right|subscript|superscript](...)` work as high-level methods to build `stringPict` and `prettyForm` complex objects, leaving `stringPict.[stack|next]()` methods as low-level methods. High-level methods then return objects of the same type as the caller object (`stringPict|prettyForm`), while low-level methods return tuples of `(str, int)`. 
The use of the high-level methods allows us to avoid the idiom `prettyForm(*stringPict.method(...))`, which is spread all over Sympy. This behavior seems to be suggested by the implementation of `stringPict.root`, a method that didn't work in the master branch but does it here. 

Another change included is to support horizontal and vertical alignment in text blocks, which was marked as a TODO task, and which also helps to build complex stringPicts without explicitly computing the baselines on the building routines. 

The implementation of `stringPict.root`, which is working now, suggests that this organization was considered before.

#### Other comments

I tried to modify the rest of the code as little as possible to play with my project without breaking tests. I have more suggestions for improving the pretty module, but could be go in another PR.


#### Release notes

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->

* printing
  * the methods  `subscript` and `superscript` were added to the `stringPict` interface
  * the methods `above`, `below`, `left`, `right` and `parens` were modified to return `prettyForm` or `stringPict`
   objects depending on the class of the object over which are called.
   * `above`, `below`, `left`,  and `right`  now support a keyword argument `align` that allows to control the relative alignment of the elements.
   * the `root` method was fixed, and used to simplify the code in `PrettyPrinter._print_nth_root()`.
   * doctests for the modified methods were added.
   * tests and the use of the modified methods were adjusted along the code.

<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

API","user":{"login":"mmatera","id":2862288,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4NjIyODg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2862288?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera","html_url":"https://github.com/mmatera","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"#### References to other Issues or PRs

I am working on giving Mathics (https://github.com/Mathics3/mathics-core/pull/1155) the ability to produce prettyprint
-like output from Mathics `Expression` objects. To avoid duplicating efforts, I tried to hook on the `sympy.printing.pretty` package, but I found some problems. 
After trying to use the `PrettyPrinter`, I realized that since `mathics.core.Expression` objects behave differently than Sympy objects, the best would be to generate text blocks directly from the `sympy.printing.pretty.stringpict.prettyForm` class. A draft of it can be seen at https://github.com/Mathics3/mathics-core/pull/1162

In this journey, I found some issues in the design of `sympy.printing.pretty.stringpict` that makes me difficult to use it directly on my project. 
This PR includes the changes I need to make it work. Also, it probably helps with #20894.

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In this PR I propose some changes in the `stringPict` API, which I hope will make it easier to use it both inside Sympy  or in in other projects, like Mathics.

* the methods  `subscript` and `superscript` were added to the `stringPict` interface
* the methods `above`, `below`, `left`, `right` and `parens` were modified to return `prettyForm` or `stringPict`
   objects depending on the class of the object over which are called.
* `above`, `below`, `left`,  and `right`  now support a keyword argument `align` that allows to control the relative alignment of the elements.
* the `root` method was fixed, and used to simplify the code in `PrettyPrinter._print_nth_root()`.
* doctests for the modified methods were added.
* tests and the use of the modified methods were adjusted along the code.

The idea is that `stringPict.[above|below,left|right|subscript|superscript](...)` work as high-level methods to build `stringPict` and `prettyForm` complex objects, leaving `stringPict.[stack|next]()` methods as low-level methods. High-level methods then return objects of the same type as the caller object (`stringPict|prettyForm`), while low-level methods return tuples of `(str, int)`. 
The use of the high-level methods allows us to avoid the idiom `prettyForm(*stringPict.method(...))`, which is spread all over Sympy. This behavior seems to be suggested by the implementation of `stringPict.root`, a method that didn't work in the master branch but does it here. 

Another change included is to support horizontal and vertical alignment in text blocks, which was marked as a TODO task, and which also helps to build complex stringPicts without explicitly computing the baselines on the building routines. 

The implementation of `stringPict.root`, which is working now, suggests that this organization was considered before.

#### Other comments

I tried to modify the rest of the code as little as possible to play with my project without breaking tests. I have more suggestions for improving the pretty module, but could be go in another PR.


#### Release notes

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->

* printing
  * the methods  `subscript` and `superscript` were added to the `stringPict` interface
  * the methods `above`, `below`, `left`, `right` and `parens` were modified to return `prettyForm` or `stringPict`
   objects depending on the class of the object over which are called.
   * `above`, `below`, `left`,  and `right`  now support a keyword argument `align` that allows to control the relative alignment of the elements.
   * the `root` method was fixed, and used to simplify the code in `PrettyPrinter._print_nth_root()`.
   * doctests for the modified methods were added.
   * tests and the use of the modified methods were adjusted along the code.

API","user":{"login":"mmatera","id":2862288,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4NjIyODg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2862288?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera","html_url":"https://github.com/mmatera","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":84627347,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw4NDYyNzM0Nw==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/labels/printing","name":"printing","color":"0000FF","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":15,"created_at":"2024-11-13T12:19:34Z","updated_at":"2024-11-13T19:39:14Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/pulls/27257","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/27257","diff_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/27257.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/27257.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"#### References to other Issues or PRs

I am working on giving Mathics (https://github.com/Mathics3/mathics-core/pull/1155) the ability to produce prettyprint
-like output from Mathics `Expression` objects. To avoid duplicating efforts, I tried to hook on the `sympy.printing.pretty` package, but I found some problems. 
After trying to use the `PrettyPrinter`, I realized that since `mathics.core.Expression` objects behave differently than Sympy objects, the best would be to generate text blocks directly from the `sympy.printing.pretty.stringpict.prettyForm` class. A draft of it can be seen at https://github.com/Mathics3/mathics-core/pull/1162

In this journey, I found some issues in the design of `sympy.printing.pretty.stringpict` that makes me difficult to use it directly on my project. 
This PR includes the changes I need to make it work. Also, it probably helps with #20894.

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In this PR I propose some changes in the `stringPict` API, which I hope will make it easier to use it both inside Sympy  or in in other projects, like Mathics.

* the methods  `subscript` and `superscript` were added to the `stringPict` interface
* the methods `above`, `below`, `left`, `right` and `parens` were modified to return `prettyForm` or `stringPict`
   objects depending on the class of the object over which are called.
* `above`, `below`, `left`,  and `right`  now support a keyword argument `align` that allows to control the relative alignment of the elements.
* the `root` method was fixed, and used to simplify the code in `PrettyPrinter._print_nth_root()`.
* doctests for the modified methods were added.
* tests and the use of the modified methods were adjusted along the code.

The idea is that `stringPict.[above|below,left|right|subscript|superscript](...)` work as high-level methods to build `stringPict` and `prettyForm` complex objects, leaving `stringPict.[stack|next]()` methods as low-level methods. High-level methods then return objects of the same type as the caller object (`stringPict|prettyForm`), while low-level methods return tuples of `(str, int)`. 
The use of the high-level methods allows us to avoid the idiom `prettyForm(*stringPict.method(...))`, which is spread all over Sympy. This behavior seems to be suggested by the implementation of `stringPict.root`, a method that didn't work in the master branch but does it here. 

Another change included is to support horizontal and vertical alignment in text blocks, which was marked as a TODO task, and which also helps to build complex stringPicts without explicitly computing the baselines on the building routines. 

The implementation of `stringPict.root`, which is working now, suggests that this organization was considered before.

#### Other comments

I tried to modify the rest of the code as little as possible to play with my project without breaking tests. I have more suggestions for improving the pretty module, but could be go in another PR.


#### Release notes

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->

* printing
  * the methods  `subscript` and `superscript` were added to the `stringPict` interface
  * the methods `above`, `below`, `left`, `right` and `parens` were modified to return `prettyForm` or `stringPict`
   objects depending on the class of the object over which are called.
   * `above`, `below`, `left`,  and `right`  now support a keyword argument `align` that allows to control the relative alignment of the elements.
   * the `root` method was fixed, and used to simplify the code in `PrettyPrinter._print_nth_root()`.
   * doctests for the modified methods were added.
   * tests and the use of the modified methods were adjusted along the code.

<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

Basically, the pretty printer should keep track of the way the expressions were built up, so that you basically have a symbolic representation of the blocks of printed expressions. This would make reasoning about certain more complex printing situations easier because you could look inside what has already been printed. So I would consider whether the APIs proposed here could be adapted to that (if not now, then at some point in the future).

Another suggestion I have here is to use operator overloading for left, right, above, and below, like maybe `a + b` and `a/b`. Although I'm uncertain if this might cause confusion with actual mathematical expressions. At the same time, something like [this](https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/27257/files#diff-3fd23f9a6c4768c78481c24d66a4810ea7ec1ca3b81bfe78159405eb6cb3c3a5R174) is very confusing, particularly because the `left` method is "backwards". References to other Issues or PRs

I am working on giving Mathics (https://github.com/Mathics3/mathics-core/pull/1155) the ability to produce prettyprint
-like output from Mathics `Expression` objects. To avoid duplicating efforts, I tried to hook on the `sympy.printing.pretty` package, but I found some problems. 
After trying to use the `PrettyPrinter`, I realized that since `mathics.core.Expression` objects behave differently than Sympy objects, the best would be to generate text blocks directly from the `sympy.printing.pretty.stringpict.prettyForm` class. A draft of it can be seen at https://github.com/Mathics3/mathics-core/pull/1162

In this journey, I found some issues in the design of `sympy.printing.pretty.stringpict` that makes me difficult to use it directly on my project. 
This PR includes the changes I need to make it work. Also, it probably helps with #20894.

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In this PR I propose some changes in the `stringPict` API, which I hope will make it easier to use it both inside Sympy  or in in other projects, like Mathics.

* the methods  `subscript` and `superscript` were added to the `stringPict` interface
* the methods `above`, `below`, `left`, `right` and `parens` were modified to return `prettyForm` or `stringPict`
   objects depending on the class of the object over which are called.
* `above`, `below`, `left`,  and `right`  now support a keyword argument `align` that allows to control the relative alignment of the elements.
* the `root` method was fixed, and used to simplify the code in `PrettyPrinter._print_nth_root()`.
* doctests for the modified methods were added.
* tests and the use of the modified methods were adjusted along the code.

The idea is that `stringPict.[above|below,left|right|subscript|superscript](...)` work as high-level methods to build `stringPict` and `prettyForm` complex objects, leaving `stringPict.[stack|next]()` methods as low-level methods. High-level methods then return objects of the same type as the caller object (`stringPict|prettyForm`), while low-level methods return tuples of `(str, int)`. 
The use of the high-level methods allows us to avoid the idiom `prettyForm(*stringPict.method(...))`, which is spread all over Sympy. This behavior seems to be suggested by the implementation of `stringPict.root`, a method that didn't work in the master branch but does it here. 

Another change included is to support horizontal and vertical alignment in text blocks, which was marked as a TODO task, and which also helps to build complex stringPicts without explicitly computing the baselines on the building routines. 

The implementation of `stringPict.root`, which is working now, suggests that this organization was considered before.

#### Other comments

I tried to modify the rest of the code as little as possible to play with my project without breaking tests. I have more suggestions for improving the pretty module, but could be go in another PR.


#### Release notes

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->

* printing
  * the methods  `subscript` and `superscript` were added to the `stringPict` interface
  * the methods `above`, `below`, `left`, `right` and `parens` were modified to return `prettyForm` or `stringPict`
   objects depending on the class of the object over which are called.
   * `above`, `below`, `left`,  and `right`  now support a keyword argument `align` that allows to control the relative alignment of the elements.
   * the `root` method was fixed, and used to simplify the code in `PrettyPrinter._print_nth_root()`.
   * doctests for the modified methods were added.
   * tests and the use of the modified methods were adjusted along the code.

<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

If the conclusion is the latter, then the question is how much we need to be refactoring stringPict itself instead. 

> I found it a little bit strange that the __add__ operator between two stringPicts produces a new stringPict with a "+" in the middle, and handles the logic of parenthesis. I would expect that it means block-wise concatenation (like next does, or + does for str objects), but OK, it does not bother too much.

I did not even realize this was the case. It's definitely trying to do too many things at once, or more specifically, it is trying to cover too many different levels of abstraction at the same time. It should either be low level, and not know anything about mathematics, just combining boxes together, or higher level and know something about how mathematical functions are printed. 

It seems like there are a lot of issues with stringPict. Even the name isn't really up to PEP 8 standards. It's a really old piece of code; one of the oldest in SymPy. So the question is whether we should just abandon it and rewrite it to something better, or, if we decide we don't consider it to be a public API, just completely refactor it and fix these issue. 

I like Oscar's way of framing it as it should be a sequence of conversions, like a compiler. I think the most important thing we need to decide here is just how public the stringPict API is. If we consider it to be nonpublic, then we should feel free to heavily refactor or even delete it. As long as the existing pretty printing tests continue to pass, or if the results do change they aren't an improvement. 

If we decide it is public API, then we should just leave it be and start a separate submodule that's independent of it. 

My understanding was that this PR does have some API breaks to stringPict (correct?), so it would be good to figure this out.

It looks like galgebra uses stringPict https://github.com/pygae/galgebra/blob/327df0a1f9a2e338efa5ce373ce2abc1af0fc874/galgebra/atoms.py#L9. I didn't spot anything else major using it, just a few minor repos. But I think it's fair to say that *someone* out there depends on it. 

It's also mostly independent from the rest of SymPy so in principle someone could just copy the code if they still want to use it. there,

I'm working with an [algorithm](https://github.com/SymposiumOrganization/NeuralSymbolicRegressionThatScales) that relies on calling `sympy.lambdify` hundreds of millions of times (~200M) and noticed the memory usage of the process steadily creeping up and eventually crashing the program.

I was able to trace the problem to the following (see **Demo** below).

Notably, the memory usage still increases despite
1. lambdifying the same equation again
2. "deleting" the lambdified equation

**System Information:**
WSL2 (Windows 10.0.22631 Build 22631)

```
> lsb_release -a
No LSB modules are available.
Distributor ID: Ubuntu
Description:    Ubuntu 22.04.3 LTS
Release:        22.04
Codename:       jammy
```
```
> python --version
Python 3.11.10
```
```
> pip freeze
mpmath==1.3.0
psutil==6.1.0
sympy==1.13.3
```


### Demo:


```python
# sympy_memory.py
import argparse
import psutil

import sympy


if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Parse command line arguments
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Run equation n times')
    parser.add_argument('-n', type=int, default=1, help='Number of repetitions')
    args = parser.parse_args()

    # Get the current process to monitor memory usage
    process = psutil.Process()

    # Parse an example equation
    equation_str = 'x'
    parsed_equation = sympy.sympify(equation_str)

    print("Memory Usage:")

    for i in range(args.n):
        # Lambdifying the (same) equation in each iteration
        lambdified_equation = sympy.lambdify('x', parsed_equation)

        # Print memory usage every 10000 iterations
        if i % 10000 == 0:
            mem_usage = process.memory_info().rss / 1024 / 1024  # Convert to MB
            print(f"{i}/{args.n}: {mem_usage:.2f} MB")

        # "Deleting" the lambdified equation
        del lambdified_equation
```

```
> python sympy_memory.py -n 100000
Memory Usage:
0/100000: 49.91 MB
10000/100000: 54.29 MB
20000/100000: 59.27 MB
30000/100000: 64.17 MB
40000/100000: 68.04 MB
50000/100000: 72.05 MB
60000/100000: 75.91 MB
70000/100000: 79.78 MB
80000/100000: 83.45 MB
90000/100000: 89.08 MB
```

**Observation:** The memory usage increases by about ~~+390KB~~ (correction: 390B https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27216#issuecomment-2453168997) per lambdified equation, totalling +39MB over 100k lambdifications and deletions.
