{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27116","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27116/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27116/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27116/events","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27116","id":2554180928,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAnGFs6YPbVA","number":27116,"title":"Use hatchling as a build backend","user":{"login":"oscarbenjamin","id":1159732,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNTk3MzI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1159732?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin","html_url":"https://github.com/oscarbenjamin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":21,"created_at":"2024-09-28T11:48:31Z","updated_at":"2024-10-23T18:43:45Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"COLLABORATOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Although SymPy now has a pyproject.toml it is missing the `project` table. See:

https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/tutorials/packaging-projects/

This means that various packaging frontend tools will use setuptools as the build backend as specified in PEP 517:

https://peps.python.org/pep-0517/

> If the pyproject.toml file is absent, or the build-backend key is missing, the source tree is not using this specification, and tools should revert to the legacy behaviour of running setup.py (either directly, or by implicitly invoking the `setuptools.build_meta:__legacy__` backend).

The first thing that should be done now for SymPy is at least to add the project section in pyproject.toml specifying setuptools as the build backend and then remove setup.py altogether. This is now the modern expected way to use setuptools for building a Python project:

https://setuptools.pypa.io/en/latest/userguide/quickstart.html

Actually though I think that it would be better to migrate to a different build backend rather than setuptools. There are a number of alternatives here but I think that for SymPy the best option is hatchling. Configuring the use of hatchling is described here:

https://hatch.pypa.io/latest/config/build/

Note that hatchling is associated with the hatch project frontend tool but the point of PEP 517 is that you should be able to freely mix and match frontends and backends. In other words you can use whatever pip, uv, hatch, conda etc frontends regardless of what the build backend is. Currently the SymPy codebase is not fully compatible with e.g. using uv as a frontend tool (for anything more than just installation) because it lacks the project section in pyproject.toml though.

There are alternatives to hatchling as a build backend such as flit, poetry-core, pdm-backend, meson-python, scikit-build etc and obviously setuptools. This is my summary of the differences:

- setuptools. It is still fine to use setuptools but other backends have made improvements over setuptools while setuptools itself cannot really improve easily because of backwards compatibility constraints. Use of setuptools is widespread but I would not recommend it for new projects or as a desirable option among these.

- flit. The flit backend is deliberately minimal and basic. I believe it lacks some basic features like editable installs that we would want for SymPy.

- poetry-core is I think quite tied to poetry itself which I would not recommend for SymPy. I think poetry makes more sense for projects that are applications rather than libraries and that want to do things like pinning all dependencies etc.

- pdm-backend is again tied to the pdm project frontend much like poetry. Probably pdm would be a better choice for frontend but it is not so widely used and I expect that it will not last in the future of Python packaging.

- meson-python and scikit-build are for building more complex projects with non-Python modules and dependencies. For example python-flint now uses meson-python which brings a massive improvement in build configuration and development UX. These are much more complicated though then the other backends that are mainly designed for Python-only projects.

- hatchling. This is the default recommendation in the Python packaging user guide. Besides setuptools, hatchling is the only build backend that is maintained under the PyPA (Python packaging authority) umbrella. It is also the default recommended by uv when creating new Python projects. I have tested it out elsewhere and found that it works well.

One reason that I would like to use hatchling as backend is so that I can use the `hatch version` command to bump versions and manage the version packaging metadata and to single source the version. Note though that using hatchling as a build backend does not imply that anyone needs to use hatch as a frontend either to install sympy or to do development work.

We could separately consider whether or not to make use of hatch as a development frontend. In that role it can do the equivalent of what tools like nox can do e.g. to manage virtual environments for running tests, building docs etc. This would mean that you could do e.g.
```
hatch run docs:build
```
which would either reuse create a virtual environment if needed, install all of the dependencies needed for building the docs and then build the docs. This issue is not about using hatch as a development frontend though and is just about adding the project section in pyproject.toml and using hatchling instead of setuptools as the build backend.

I have some experience with using each of these backends (except pdm-backend) and have concluded that hatchling is the better choice for sympy.

Adding the project table and using hatchling as a build backend basically amounts to adding this in pyproject.toml (a bit more is needed to define exactly which files to include/exclude):
```toml
[project]
name = "sympy"
version = "1.14.0"
authors = [
  # ...
]
description = "Computer algebra system"
readme = "README.md"
requires-python = ">=3.8"
classifiers = [
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3",
    "License :: OSI Approved :: MIT License",
    "Operating System :: OS Independent",
]

[build-system]
requires = ["hatchling"]
build-backend = "hatchling.build"

[tool.hatch.build]

# Files included in sdist:
include = [
    "sympy",
    "LICENSE",
    "AUTHORS",
    # ...
]

[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]

# Files included in wheel:
packages = [
    "sympy",
]
```
Then any installer that can install from sdist or VCS checkout should be able to install using this metadata. Likewise any PEP 517 frontend can be used to build sdist and wheel.

I think that most SymPy contributors would not notice the build backend. If you install using e.g. `pip install [--editable] .` for development then the hatchling requirement gets installed into a temporary venv behind the scenes (just as setuptools does now). If you run these same commands without build isolation then you would need to e.g. `pip install hatchling` first. This is also true of setuptools for newer Python versions though: it is not included by default any more just as distutils has been removed altogether.

We can delete the setup.py altogether once we have these sections in pyproject.toml. An alternative would be to continue to use setuptools as build backend but migrate everything from setup.py to pyproject.toml to use the modern way to configure setuptools. I don't see any advantage in continuing to use setuptools any more though and migrating from setup.py to pyproject.toml is just as much work either way.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27116/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":3,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27116/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2433153187","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27116#issuecomment-2433153187","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27116","id":2433153187,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs6RBvij","user":{"login":"asmeurer","id":71486,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcxNDg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/71486?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer","html_url":"https://github.com/asmeurer","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-23T18:43:43Z","updated_at":"2024-10-23T18:43:43Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Surely someone's done this before. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/24976","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/24976/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/24976/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/24976/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/24976","id":1955546731,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP850j0Zr","number":24976,"title":"ENH: Ensure that output of np.clip has the same dtype as the main array","user":{"login":"mhvk","id":2789820,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3ODk4MjA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2789820?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk","html_url":"https://github.com/mhvk","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mhvk/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":17,"created_at":"2023-10-21T15:50:34Z","updated_at":"2024-10-23T19:18:52Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

(Discussed in https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/23912#discussion_r1365994920)

The function `np.clip` arguably has surprising casting behaviour:
```
a = np.arange(5, dtype='u1')
np.clip(a, -1, 3)
# OverflowError with NEP 50
np.clip(a, np.int64(-1), np.int64(3))
# array([0, 1, 2, 3, 3])  
# int64 dtype with NEP 50
# (before NEP 50, both examples gave int16)
```
I would naively have expected for the output dtype to always be the same as the input one. That this does not happen is because internally `np.clip` calls a ufunc: https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/d885b0b546cbe2ab8401622c8d4db984ec5ea31b/numpy/_core/_methods.py#L92-L101
and these treat the arguments symmetrically.

It is possible to get the output dtype by setting `out` or `dtype`, but in the current implementation that still gives either the `OverflowError` or casting errors:
```
np.clip(a, np.int64(-1), np.int64(3), out=a)  # or dtype=a.dtype
# UFuncTypeError: Cannot cast ufunc 'clip' output from dtype('int64') to dtype('uint8') with casting rule 'same_kind'
```
adding `casting="unsafe"` gives the wrong answer, because `-1` becomes `255`.

I think it should be possible to make the `np.clip` function (probably not the `ufunc`) cast the `min` and `max` to `a.dtype`, but ensure that the valid ranges are respected (i.e., negative integers would become 0 if the dtype is unsigned). This would be similar to what was done in #24915, i.e., ideally we have the behaviour of `np.clip` be identical to
```
min = -1
max = 3
out = a.copy()
out[a<min] = min
out[a>max] = max
return out
```
(which still gives an out-of-bound error for `min=-1` because of `__setitem__`, but works for `min=np.int64(-1)`)

But perhaps this is more work than is warranted.

Really it would be nice if there were a codecov like service for doing benchmarks on CI that could handle these sorts of things for us, but I don't know of any. So it seems that dummification is happening (although I don't see the flag being passed through in the code. Has the default for lambdify actually changed? It still says `dummify=False` in the signature)

As others have noted, the issue ise symbols that were never provided at all. 

This is also arguable an issue with lambdify, although one that's existed for a long time so I don't know if it will be easy to change. IMO if 'sympy' is not in the lambdify modules lambdify should not allow you to lambdify an expression that contains symbols that aren't specified as input variables (or at the very least give a warning). This is almost always indicates an error:

```
>>> import numpy as np
>>> lambdify(x, x + y, 'numpy')(np.arange(10))
array([y, y + 1, y + 2, y + 3, y + 4, y + 5, y + 6, y + 7, y + 8, y + 9],
      dtype=object)
```

(*almost*, though, because I'm sure there are actually people relying on this behavior).

Certainly in nsolve you don't want it because obviously mpmath.findroot isn't going to work with a function that returns symbolic SymPy expressions.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2327487067/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

that this only enables dependabot for GitHub Actions workflow versions (e.g., things like `actions/checkout@v3`). This does not enable it for things like Python packages. We could do that later, but that's a separate discussion.

This will run dependabot one a week (I believe on Mondays). The updates are grouped, so there should only be at most one PR.

I have also added a workflow that enables auto-merge on these PRs, so that if the checks pass, they will merge automatically. We can remove this if you want to merge them manually, but I've found that the actions updates rarely break anything so this just makes it so you can ignore dependabot.

I'm only a new and indirect user via the PySR library.

In PySR, expressions using log2 are translated to SymPy via `sympy.log(x, 2)`. This is apparently forcibly translated to log(x)/log(2), and shows up in the final result in this form. However, the desired expression just is log2 directly.

To circumvent this, one can instead translate log2 to `sympy.codegen.cfunctions.log2(x)`. This correctly shows up as log2 in the resulting expressions. [Corresponding patch for PySR](https://github.com/MilesCranmer/PySR/pull/712/files)

However, `cfunctions.log2` seems to be an internal, undocumented function. Are we using the right approach? Is there a better documented way to get to the same result?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27049/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27049/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2341451467","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27049#issuecomment-2341451467","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27049","id":2341451467,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs6Lj7bL","user":{"login":"asmeurer","id":71486,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcxNDg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/71486?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer","html_url":"https://github.com/asmeurer","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-10T16:39:25Z","updated_at":"2024-09-10T16:39:25Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/6888# is also relevant here","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2341451467/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Please include a self-contained copy-pastable example that generates the issue if possible.

Please be concise with code posted. See guidelines below on how to provide a good bug report:

- Craft Minimal Bug Reports http://matthewrocklin.com/blog/work/2018/02/28/minimal-bug-reports
- Minimal Complete Verifiable Examples https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve

Bug reports that follow these guidelines are easier to diagnose, and so are often handled much more quickly.
-->

**Describe the issue**:

scikit-image's CI started failing with Dask 2024.8 when it worked fine before with 2024.7. As described in https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/7491, we traced the failure 
```plain
ValueError: Array chunk size or shape is unknown. 
```
to a line that tries to index the result of a `numpy.flatnonzero` operation. See the MCVE below which fails for 2024.8 but works fine for 2024.7. 

**Minimal Complete Verifiable Example**:

```python
import numpy as np
import dask.array as da

hist = da.from_array(np.arange(256, dtype=int), chunks=(256,))
result = np.flatnonzero(hist)
result[[0, -1]]  # ValueError: Array chunk size or shape is unknown. 
```

**Anything else we need to know?**:

From reading the [docs that are pointed out by the error message](https://docs.dask.org/en/latest/array-chunks.html#unknown-chunks), it seems this behavior might not be entirely unexpected and this might not actually be a bug. However, I'm unsure why it worked before with 2024.7. And since I currently don't really now how to address this on scikit-image's side without adding an extra code path to support Dask 2024.8, I'd be grateful for feedback and context.

Sorry if I missed any existing issues or docs that already describe or explain this.

**Environment**:

- Dask version: 2024.8.0
- Python version: 3.12.0
- Operating System: Linux 6.10.3-arch1-1 (64-bit)
- Install method (conda, pip, source): pip

There have been discussions about adding array API support to Dask itself (most notably at https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/8750, and there's also https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/10994). For now, array-api-compat wraps libraries like Dask that are close to the array API but have various differences. Lazy arrays are definitely something that we care about in the array API and have tried to make allowances for. For instance, APIs in the standard that have value-dependent shapes are optional (like boolean indexing and unique). If you feel that there are issues with the array API that would prevent Dask from adopting it, please bring them up in the array-api repo. That is, don't require the user to name or even create the file. There would always just be a file, say

```
docs/CHANGES_FOR_THIS_PR.md
```

which would contain instructions and a place for the user to write their notes. Then, on merge, some CI bot automatically takes the contents of this file and moves them to the release notes page. You also could have the CI do this in the PR itself (similar to how precommit CI works), so that you aren't creating new commits on top of every merge. 

Or maybe even better: just have users edit the release notes page directly, but have a CI bot that automatically adds commits to the PR to hash out annoying merge conflicts. 

There's a lot of room for innovation here. My goal with sympy-bot was to make writing notes as dead simple as possible, so that people would actually do it. If it's a multi-step process to write notes, then either people won't do it, or we make it required and then we have to constantly bug people to do it and instruct them in how it works and so on. 

I think I wrote this somewhere above, but ideally the user should not be required to write anything in a release notes entry that cannot be obtained automatically. That includes their username, the branch name (which isn't actually relevant), or the PR number, and that includes both the contents of the note and any potential file name. 

Ultimately, it can be hard to get some people to actually write *about* their code, whether that be commit messages, release note entries, PR descriptions, docstrings, useful comments, etc. That's the fundamental problem, and there's only so much we can technically do to solve it.

Another crazy idea is to have LLM try to write release notes automatically. But that would work best when it can summarize something that was written somewhere else (PR summary, commit messages, ...), because they are usually pretty bad at just guessing from the diff, so the above problem would still be relevant.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2359183006/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

It's maybe not simpler for the person writing the tool but it is simpler for the people using it. 

> There is also room for just using standard tooling, matching the workflow of other projects etc. SymPy is hardly the first project to consider these issues so without some significant effort I don't see why we would come up with something that is significantly better than what other projects have.

I mentioned this because @skieffer mentioned writing his own tool. If you're going to write your own tool, it would be good to do it right. Another option is upstreaming improvements to towncrier or some other tool itself (it is open source after all).

> I don't see why the sympy-bot complaint about release notes is any better than a CI check that says "add a notes/xyz.txt file with release notes".

It's not about where the message lives (I agree a PR comment vs. a CI check is not that important). It's about the steps the user has to take to write the notes. Every extra step someone has to take to contribute beyond the actual code they write is friction. It also makes it harder on reviewers because they will have to walk every new contributor through the process if it is not straightforward. If we can avoid things that are technically unnecessary we should. 

The reason people don't write notes with sympy-bot is that the bot has the `NO ENTRY` escape hatch, and people do that because it's easier. People are also going to do that with towncrier or whatever other tool as long as it's an option. Again, the fundamental issue is that (a lot of) people don't like writing, and tooling cannot fix that. I do think we should remove NO ENTRY, and just have a separate "internal" category or something, so that every change has to have an entry (and we could do this pretty easily right now with sympy-bot if we wanted to).

Reviewers not reviewing the notes is also something I'm not completely convinced will change with different tooling. Reviewers are supposed to do that. If they aren't, that's a problem with our reviewing process. In fact, the whole point of sympy-bot is that reviewers can write or edit the notes themselves very easily, just by editing the PR summary. Again, this is a social (people) issue, not a technical one. Maybe people will be more adamant about reviewing the notes if they are in the diff, but I wouldn't just assume that would be the case.

To me, the main *technical* issues with sympy-bot are:

- the notes are put on the wiki, but the docs would be better (this could actually be fixed with sympy-bot itself, if we wanted)
- It's an independent system that we have to maintain, and it would be better to use something off the shelf that someone else maintains.

```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/13828/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/13828/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2362021116","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/13828#issuecomment-2362021116","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/13828","id":2362021116,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs6MyZT8","user":{"login":"asmeurer","id":71486,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcxNDg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/71486?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer","html_url":"https://github.com/asmeurer","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/asmeurer/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-19T19:30:20Z","updated_at":"2024-09-19T19:30:20Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"There is already FunctionMatrix in the matrix expressions:

```py
>>> FunctionMatrix(10**3, 10**3, Lambda((i, j), Piecewise((1, Eq(i, j)), (0, True))))
FunctionMatrix(1000, 1000, Lambda((i, j), Piecewise((1, Eq(i, j)), (0, True))))
```

Of course, matrix expressions already have Identity directly:

```py
>>> Identity(10**3)
I
```

Matrix expressions are a much better approach to this problem because they can represent the expression symbolically, meaning it's possible to construct further matrices without evaluating. They are also unevaluated by default meaning you don't have to worry about an operation accidentally trying to evaluate a million matrix entries. was changed. Do not include the issue number in the title. -->



#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

Avoid page numbers like this in TOC from PDF:

![Capture d’écran 2024-07-27 à 13 27 13](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d7076609-8a2d-4fdf-8227-4b95b65d80c4)

or like this

![Capture d’écran 2024-07-27 à 13 27 21](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a061e291-968f-4d02-b91c-45670080687e)

and try to avoid (untested because I did not build the PDF docs locally; but we do use that for our (Sphinx) own docs) things like that in index:

![Capture d’écran 2024-07-27 à 13 29 33](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4e57f1cd-9967-4672-928e-7f9af055d8df)

Not sure the index will be free of all such glitches but it can only improve it.

Screenshots on 1.13.1 PDF docs as available today.

#### Other comments

None

#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage
and the release note for this PR. For example:

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units

During build of docs via Sphinx 8 (localized to French).
```
Conversion de  `source_suffix = '.rst'` en `source_suffix = {'.rst': 'restructuredtext'}`.
```

The `source_suffix` Sphinx configuration expects a dict and accepts a string only for backward compatibility.

cf https://github.com/sphinx-doc/sphinx/pull/12584.

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

Suitable LaTeX font configuration to support all needed characters.

#### Other comments

We only need two additional fonts from a standard LaTeX installation based on TeXLive.  But rather than go check how this is packaged in Ubuntu or on Windows, this is done is completely safe way and will be enacted only if the font are actually there.

Most characters where already available in DejaVu Sans, but not in DejaVu Sans Mono. 

A mark-up glitch in a source found while examining PDF was also fixed.
